_comment: Keyword reminders for your Familiar. This is data, not code: edit it to add your own triggers, no Python needed. Each trigger has: skill (a unique name, used for dedup), label (optional, the tag shown in brackets; defaults to SKILL-TRIGGER), built (true once the thing exists), keywords (lowercase words or short phrases that fire it, matched on word boundaries), and reminder (the line your Familiar sees when a keyword matches). These nudges exist so your Familiar actually reaches for the right skill in the moment, instead of improvising.

triggers.reminder: Keep the status board absolutely current. Read status-board.md before answering anything about active work, and the moment a tracked client, project, task, or thread changes state, update its row the same turn (move resolved rows to archived). Use the status-board-update skill. A stale board is worse than none.

This is inbox work. Use the email-triage skill, which works through your claude.ai mail connector: sort what needs them, draft the replies that do not, and surface anything load-bearing. Read the relevant person note before drafting anything addressed to someone.
Use the daily-brief skill: read the status board, calendar, and inbox, and give one tight brief of what matters today.
Use the weekly-review skill: sweep the status board, name what moved and what stalled, and set up the week ahead.

Driving the browser is your real power. When a task lives on a website with no API, use the cdp-usage skill to drive Chrome: find the element, fill it with real key events, click, and confirm with a screenshot. Work in the background and never steal your person's screen.

Use the connector-setup skill to walk your person through the claude.ai web path for a new connector (Settings then Connectors, then a window reload here). Never tell them to run command-line tooling.
Use the voice-capture skill to collect their writing samples and distil how they actually write into a working voice profile, before you publish anything under their name.
Use the self-improve skill: capture what slowed you down or what you got wrong, codify the fix as a rule, so the same friction does not repeat.
Your person may be stating a durable rule. Use the pattern-codify skill to file it in your knowledge corpus this same turn.

If your person wants a feature you do not have, hits something broken, or asks how you work in a way your own docs do not answer, use the give-feedback skill to send it to the EcodiaOS team at code@ecodia.au. Do not invent a capability or a roadmap; pass the real request through.
Use the pack-update skill to fetch upstream doctrine safely.
